title: Average ETF Expense Ratio (2026): What You Should Actuall...
description: Discover the real average ETF expense ratio in 2026. Compare Vanguard, Schwab, and active vs passive funds to see if you are overpaying in fees.

mainEntity.name: What is a good expense ratio for an ETF?

acceptedAnswer.text: A 'good' expense ratio depends heavily on the category. For a broad passive index fund (like the S&P 500), a good ratio is under 0.05%. For actively managed funds or niche thematic ETFs, anything under 0.50% is generally considered competitive.

mainEntity.name: What is the average ETF expense ratio in 2026?

acceptedAnswer.text: Based on our 2026 analysis of over 100 major ETFs, the overall average expense ratio is 0.40%, with a median of 0.27%. Passive index funds average significantly lower.

mainEntity.name: Why are Vanguard expense ratios so low?

acceptedAnswer.text: Vanguard operates with a unique corporate structure where the company is owned by its funds, which are in turn owned by the investors. This structural design means profits are essentially returned to investors in the form of consistently lower expense ratios.

mainEntity.name: Are zero expense ratio ETFs worth it?

acceptedAnswer.text: Zero expense ratio funds can be excellent for long-term investors, as every basis point saved compounds over time. However, investors should also consider the fund's tracking error, bid-ask spread, and potential capital gains distributions, as fees are not the only cost of ownership.

mainEntity.name: How do ETF expense ratios differ from mutual funds?

acceptedAnswer.text: On average, ETFs have lower expense ratios than mutual funds. This is largely because ETFs are more tax-efficient and have lower structural overhead. Furthermore, the ETF market has seen a far more aggressive 'fee war' among issuers over the past decade.
